line-height: 80%;">This diagram illustrates the layers of ASM storage and how each builds on the one below it. First, a raw block device must be provisioned by the storage team.&nbsp;</font><br><br><font style="font-size: 15px; line-height: 80%;">Second, that block device is marked as an ASM disk, making it visible to the ASM instance.&nbsp;</font><br><br><font style="font-size: 15px; line-height: 80%;">Third, the ASM disk is assigned to a disk group, which is the logical storage pool the database actually uses.&nbsp;</font><br><br><font style="font-size: 15px; line-height: 80%;">Each layer depends on the one beneath it without a provisioned block device there is no ASM disk, and without an ASM disk there is no disk group.</font></i></div>" vertex="1"> <mxGeometry height="170" width="480" x="380" y="610" as="geometry" /> </mxCell> </root> </mxGraphModel> </diagram> </mxfile>

i.font: This diagram illustrates the layers of ASM storage and how each builds on the one below it. First, a raw block device must be provisioned by the storage team.
Second, that block device is marked as an ASM disk, making it visible to the ASM instance.
Third, the ASM disk is assigned to a disk group, which is the logical storage pool the database actually uses.
Each layer depends on the one beneath it without a provisioned block device there is no ASM disk, and without an ASM disk there is no disk group.
